2. TERM AND PROBATION
This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue indefinitely thereafter,
subject to earlier termination as provided herein. The initial three (3) months following the Effective
Date shall constitute a probation period (the "Probation Period"). During the Probation Period, either
Party may terminate this Agreement for any reason upon providing fourteen (14) calendar days'
written notice to the other Party. Following the Probation Period, the termination provisions of
Section 11 shall apply.
3. COMPENSATION
The Client shall pay the Service Provider compensation for the Services rendered as separately

8. REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES
The Service Provider represents and warrants that he has the necessary skills, experience, and
qualifications to perform the Services in a professional and workmanlike manner, in accordance
with generally accepted industry standards. The Service Provider further represents that the
Services will not infringe upon the intellectual property rights or other rights of any third party.

strictReview.summary: The clause effectively secures intellectual property rights for the Client by combining a 'work made for hire' designation with a robust backup assignment clause.
observations: All work product created by the Service Provider is designated as 'work made for hire' to the fullest extent permitted by law.
The clause includes an explicit, irrevocable backup assignment of all right, title, and interest in the Work Product to the Client if it does not qualify as a work made for hire.
evidence: Where the creator is an independent contractor, the work created must be commissioned for the exclusive use of the client for the works made for hire doctrine to apply.
Once a work is considered one made for hire, the authorship and copyright ownership belong to the employer or the person or entity who commissioned the work of the independent contractor.

citations.title: Copyright Law: The “Works Made For Hire” Doctrine

strictReview.internalReasoning: Under US Copyright law, independent contractor work only qualifies as 'work made for hire' under narrow statutory categories. The inclusion of a backup assignment clause is a standard and highly effective legal mechanism to ensure the Client owns the IP regardless of the 'work made for hire' status, resulting in low risk.

clauseText:

9. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY
To the maximum extent permitted by applicable law, neither Party shall be liable to the other for any
indirect, incidental, special, consequential, or punitive damages, including but not limited to lost
profits, arising out of or relating to this Agreement, even if advised of the possibility of such
damages. The total liability of either Party under this Agreement shall not exceed the total
compensation paid or payable to the Service Provider by the Client under this Agreement during
the six (6) months immediately preceding the event giving rise to the claim.
10. INDEMNIFICATION

The clause does not exclude indemnification obligations (such as third-party IP infringement claims) or breaches of confidentiality from the liability cap.
evidence: A limitation of liability clause limits the amount and/or types of damages that may be attributable to a particular party under the contract for that party's future breach, misconduct while performing under the contract, or indemnification liability.

strictReview.internalReasoning: While limitation of liability clauses are generally enforceable under US law, capping liability at only 6 months of compensation without carving out critical liabilities like IP indemnification or confidentiality breaches creates a significant financial risk for the Client, as the cap may be far lower than actual damages.

strictReview.internalReasoning: The provided search results (Wikipedia and USA.gov) do not contain relevant legal authority on choice of law clauses. Based solely on the contract text, leaving the governing law blank or 'to be determined' creates a critical risk. In the event of a dispute, there is no agreed-upon law, which will lead to costly jurisdictional battles and the application of unpredictable default conflict-of-laws rules.
